title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community sports day. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Marta, The Registration Coordinator
Marta first volunteered for the sports day because she wanted local families to meet somewhere other than shops and school gates. She was unexpectedly placed at registration, which sounded simple until the first teams arrived. [47]Just before the opening race, she found that several children had names on the list but no signed permission forms.[/47] Rather than send them away, she set up a phone corner and calmly helped parents confirm details. [48]Her human-resources job helped her organise volunteers into clear roles, so nobody drifted between the finish line, water table and information tent.[/48] Marta also created spare name labels when the printed ones ran out. She says the best registration desk is invisible, because people only notice it when it fails. By lunchtime, she could identify families by the trainers left beside their picnic blankets, and the field felt less like a crowd than a temporary neighbourhood with whistles.
texts.B:
### Karim, The First-Aid Tent Helper
Karim immediately joined after a volunteer had helped his daughter during the previous year’s event, when she fell during a relay and panicked more than she was hurt. [49]That kindness to his daughter was the reason he offered to help in the first-aid tent.[/49] He completed a basic course before the sports day and expected the tent to deal with dramatic injuries. [50]Instead, he discovered that most visitors needed reassurance, shade, water or a quiet minute away from the noise.[/50] Karim quickly learned to kneel so that small children did not feel examined from above unnecessarily. He also kept spare hair ties, tissues and plasters decorated with cartoon animals. The work was tiring because he had to stay alert while nothing serious happened. He says preventing panic is difficult to measure, but parents’ shoulders dropped when they saw a calm adult waiting with clean hands, cool cloths and careful questions.
texts.C:
### Elsie, The Inclusive Games Designer
Elsie designed several of the games after a teacher asked whether the sports day could be less focused on speed and obvious winners. She personally loved competition, but she knew not every child enjoyed being ranked. [51]After a trial obstacle race left the smallest children waiting too long for their turn, she changed it into short team challenges where everyone moved at once.[/51] The new version looked much less tidy, but laughter replaced the bored waiting line. Elsie also used foam equipment, wide lanes, bright cones and rules that rewarded cooperation. [52]Her proudest moment came when a boy who usually watched from the side completed a throwing game from his wheelchair while his classmates counted aloud.[/52] She says inclusion should not mean a separate activity in the corner. It should mean designing the main shared game so more bodies can fully join without feeling like an exception or a problem.
texts.D:
### Nico, The Announcer and Timekeeper
Nico nervously took the microphone at the sports day because the event used the park where his father had trained as a runner before moving abroad. [53]That family connection to the park made him want the day to feel respectful as well as cheerful.[/53] His job involved announcing races, checking times and keeping the programme moving without sounding rushed. [54]The hardest part was adjusting the schedule when a sudden shower made the grass slippery but the hired sound system still had to be returned by five.[/54] Nico carefully filled delays with jokes, safety reminders and thanks to sponsors. [55]He also made large, numbered signs for each event area so families who did not understand the announcements could still find the right place.[/55] After the final race, he recorded the timing procedure on his phone. [56]He later turned it into notes for next year’s announcer, including which names were often mispronounced.[/56]

questions.question: discovered that some children lacked required documents just before an event began?
questions.explanation: Marta found several children had no signed permission forms before the opening race.

questions.question: used workplace experience to give volunteers clearly separated duties?
questions.explanation: Marta’s human-resources job helped her organise volunteers into clear roles.

questions.question: volunteered because someone had previously helped their child at the same event?
questions.explanation: Karim joined because a volunteer had kindly helped his daughter the year before.

questions.question: learned that the support area mostly dealt with calm and comfort rather than serious injury?
questions.explanation: Karim found most visitors needed reassurance, shade, water or quiet.

questions.question: changed an activity after realising some children spent too much time waiting?
questions.explanation: Elsie changed the obstacle race after the smallest children waited too long.

questions.question: describes a child with limited mobility taking part while classmates supported him?
questions.explanation: A boy completed a throwing game from his wheelchair while classmates counted aloud.

questions.question: wanted to help because a parent had a personal history with the location?
questions.explanation: Nico’s father had trained in the park as a runner.

questions.question: had to change the timetable because of weather and a fixed equipment deadline?
questions.explanation: Nico adjusted the schedule after rain while the sound system still had to be returned by five.

questions.question: made visual markers so people could find activities without understanding spoken information?
questions.explanation: Nico made large numbered signs for families who did not understand the announcements.

questions.question: prepared guidance for a future person doing the same announcing role?
questions.explanation: Nico made notes for next year’s announcer.
